paragraphs.text: Introductory reminder about the promise given to correspond about the spiritual life. The benefit to be expectedfrom this.

Before your departure to Moscow, we agreed to carry on a correspondence concerning the things most needful to you. Of course, I expected you would let me know how you were doing, and how you were getting settled. I have waited and waited, and am still waiting; how much time has passed, and I have yet to hear anything. Has something happened? Are you in good health? May the Mother of God keep you. Perhaps your intentions have changed? Anything is possible. That is why I am writing now, so that if that is the case, and you are concerned this correspondence would be some sort of burden on me, that you put that thought out of your head. Writing to you about these things would not be a burden for me at all; on the contrary, it would bring me no small satisfaction, and introduce some variety to my usual daily routine. Moreover, if it does not come about, then I would feel as if I had suffered a great loss. I will not try to explain how this could be, but I emphasize that it would be so.
It would not be proper to give you advice here; rather, I will make a request: write. But you should not expect any great 33

THE SPIRITUAL LIFE wisdom from me. Just a review of all that is truly worthwhile should furnish you with no small benefit, after it has revived your memory in this realm and concentrated your attention on it, perhaps, with fervor and with special energy. And just what is the good that is to be had from all this?! For, if we have problems in our lives, it is almost always not so much from ill will and a bad heart so much as it is from a lack of ardor and zeal for what is worthwhile.
